content:
GRAVITY
Gravity is the fundamental force of attraction between all masses in the universe. Newton described it mathematically in 1687 as a force proportional to the product of masses and inversely proportional to the square of the distance between them. Einstein reconceptualized gravity in 1915 not as a force but as the curvature of spacetime caused by mass and energy. Gravitational waves, ripples in spacetime predicted by Einstein, were first directly detected by LIGO in 2015 from merging black holes. Gravity governs the motion of planets, the formation of stars and galaxies, and the large-scale structure of the universe. It is the weakest of the four fundamental forces but dominates at cosmic scales because it is always attractive and has infinite range. Applications include satellite orbits, GPS systems, and understanding black holes and cosmology.

ELECTROMAGNETIC SPECTRUM
The electromagnetic spectrum encompasses all types of electromagnetic radiation ordered by wavelength and frequency. Radio waves have the longest wavelengths used for communication. Microwaves heat food and transmit data. Infrared radiation is felt as heat. Visible light is the narrow band humans see. Ultraviolet radiation causes sunburn. X-rays penetrate soft tissue for medical imaging. Gamma rays are produced by radioactive decay and cosmic events. James Clerk Maxwell unified electricity and magnetism in 1865, predicting electromagnetic waves that travel at the speed of light. Heinrich Hertz confirmed their existence in 1887. The energy of electromagnetic radiation is inversely proportional to wavelength. Applications span telecommunications, medical diagnostics, astronomy, and energy production.

QUANTUM ENTANGLEMENT
Quantum entanglement occurs when particles become correlated such that the quantum state of each particle cannot be described independently. Measuring one particle instantly determines the state of the other regardless of distance. Einstein called this spooky action at a distance and believed it revealed incompleteness in quantum mechanics. John Bell proposed in 1964 a mathematical inequality that could experimentally distinguish entanglement from local hidden variable theories. Experiments by Alain Aspect in 1982 and subsequent researchers have consistently violated Bell's inequality, confirming entanglement. Entanglement is not faster-than-light communication because no information can be transmitted. Applications include quantum computing, quantum cryptography, and quantum teleportation.

THERMODYNAMICS LAWS
The laws of thermodynamics govern energy, heat, and work. The zeroth law establishes thermal equilibrium as the basis for temperature measurement. The first law states that energy is conserved, neither created nor destroyed, only transformed. The second law states that entropy of an isolated system always increases, making perpetual motion machines impossible. The third law states that entropy approaches a constant minimum as temperature approaches absolute zero. Sadi Carnot laid the foundations in 1824 analyzing heat engine efficiency. Rudolf Clausius and Lord Kelvin formalized the laws. Thermodynamics connects physics to chemistry through reaction energetics, to biology through metabolism, and to cosmology through heat death of the universe. Applications include engine design, refrigeration, and understanding why time flows forward.

SPECIAL RELATIVITY
Einstein's special relativity published in 1905 rests on two postulates. The laws of physics are the same in all inertial reference frames. The speed of light in vacuum is constant for all observers regardless of their motion. Consequences include time dilation where moving clocks run slower, length contraction where moving objects shorten along the direction of motion, and mass-energy equivalence expressed as E equals mc squared. Relativistic effects become significant at velocities approaching the speed of light. Particle accelerators routinely confirm special relativity as particles live longer when moving at relativistic speeds. The theory replaced Newtonian mechanics for high-speed scenarios while reducing to Newtonian predictions at everyday speeds. Applications include particle physics, GPS satellite correction, and nuclear energy.

GREENHOUSE EFFECT
The greenhouse effect warms Earth's surface through atmospheric gases that trap heat. Solar radiation passes through the atmosphere and warms the surface. The Earth emits infrared radiation back toward space. Greenhouse gases including carbon dioxide, methane, and water vapor absorb and re-emit this infrared radiation, trapping heat. Without the natural greenhouse effect, Earth's average temperature would be about negative 18 degrees Celsius instead of the current 15 degrees. The enhanced greenhouse effect from human activities is causing global warming and climate change. Svante Arrhenius first calculated in 1896 that doubling atmospheric carbon dioxide would raise global temperatures. Applications include climate modeling, emissions policy, and renewable energy development.

NERVOUS SYSTEM
The nervous system coordinates the body's actions and transmits signals between different body regions. The central nervous system comprises the brain and spinal cord. The peripheral nervous system connects the central nervous system to limbs and organs. The somatic nervous system controls voluntary movement. The autonomic nervous system controls involuntary functions like heart rate, digestion, and breathing, subdivided into sympathetic fight or flight and parasympathetic rest and digest responses. Neurons are the functional units that transmit electrochemical signals. Glial cells support and protect neurons. Santiago Ramon y Cajal established the neuron doctrine in the late 19th century. Applications include neurology, psychiatry, brain-computer interfaces, and understanding consciousness.

STOMATA
Stomata are microscopic pores on plant leaf surfaces that regulate gas exchange. Guard cells flank each stoma and change shape to open or close the pore. Stomata open to allow carbon dioxide entry for photosynthesis. Stomata close to prevent water loss through transpiration. The opening and closing respond to light, carbon dioxide concentration, and water availability. Abscisic acid triggers stomatal closure during drought stress. Stomatal conductance is a key measurement in plant physiology. The evolution of stomata enabled plants to colonize land. Applications include crop breeding for drought tolerance, climate change research, and agricultural water management.

BLOOD CIRCULATION
Blood circulation transports oxygen, nutrients, hormones, and waste products throughout the body. William Harvey described the circulatory system in 1628, overturning ancient theories. The heart pumps blood through a closed system of arteries, veins, and capillaries. The right side pumps deoxygenated blood to the lungs. The left side pumps oxygenated blood to the body. The cardiac cycle includes systole when the heart contracts and diastole when it relaxes. Blood pressure is the force exerted by blood on vessel walls. Hypertension increases risk of heart disease and stroke. Red blood cells contain hemoglobin that binds oxygen. Applications include cardiovascular medicine, exercise physiology, and surgical techniques.

EARTHQUAKES
Earthquakes occur when tectonic plates suddenly slip along faults, releasing stored elastic energy as seismic waves. Primary or P-waves travel fastest through solids and liquids. Secondary or S-waves travel slower and only through solids. Surface waves cause the most damage. The Richter scale measures magnitude logarithmically. The moment magnitude scale is now preferred for large earthquakes. The epicenter is the point on the surface directly above the focus. Seismographs detect and record ground motion. The theory of elastic rebound explains how stress builds up along locked faults and releases catastrophically. Applications include building codes, early warning systems, and understanding Earth's interior structure.

IMMUNOLOGY
Immunology is the study of the immune system and its responses to pathogens and foreign substances. Innate immunity provides immediate non-specific defense. Adaptive immunity develops specific responses and immunological memory. Antibodies are proteins produced by B cells that bind to specific antigens. T cells directly kill infected cells or orchestrate immune responses. Major histocompatibility complex molecules present antigen fragments for T cell recognition. Vaccination primes the adaptive immune system against specific pathogens. Autoimmune disease occurs when self-tolerance breaks down. Applications include vaccine development, cancer immunotherapy, transplant medicine, and allergy treatment.

STARS
Stars are massive luminous spheres of plasma held together by gravity and powered by nuclear fusion. Hydrogen fuses into helium in stellar cores through the proton-proton chain or CNO cycle. The balance between gravity pulling inward and radiation pressure pushing outward determines stellar structure. Low-mass stars like the Sun become red giants then white dwarfs. High-mass stars end as supernovae leaving neutron stars or black holes. Annie Jump Cannon developed the stellar classification system OBAFGKM still used today. Cecilia Payne-Gaposchkin discovered in 1925 that stars are primarily hydrogen. Stellar nucleosynthesis produces elements up to iron during fusion and heavier elements during supernova explosions. Applications include understanding cosmic distances, exoplanet detection, and the origin of elements.

CARBON CYCLE
The carbon cycle describes the movement of carbon through Earth's atmosphere, biosphere, hydrosphere, and geosphere. Photosynthesis removes carbon dioxide from the atmosphere. Respiration and decomposition return carbon to the atmosphere. Oceans absorb and release carbon dioxide, acting as a major carbon sink. Fossil fuel combustion releases carbon sequestered over millions of years into the atmosphere over decades. Deforestation reduces carbon uptake. The carbon cycle connects biology, chemistry, geology, and climate science. Understanding the carbon cycle is critical for predicting and mitigating climate change. Applications include carbon sequestration, emissions reduction policy, and ecosystem management.

PROTEINS
Proteins are large biomolecules consisting of amino acid chains that perform vast arrays of functions in living organisms. The sequence of amino acids determines how the protein folds into its three-dimensional structure. The structure determines function. Enzymes catalyze biochemical reactions. Structural proteins like collagen provide support. Transport proteins like hemoglobin carry molecules. Signaling proteins like hormones coordinate processes. Defective protein folding causes diseases including Alzheimer's and Parkinson's. Frederick Sanger determined the first protein sequence, insulin, in 1955. Applications include drug design, industrial enzymes, and understanding genetic diseases.

RADIOACTIVITY
Radioactivity is the emission of particles or electromagnetic radiation from unstable atomic nuclei. Alpha decay emits helium nuclei. Beta decay emits electrons or positrons. Gamma decay emits high-energy photons. Half-life is the time for half of a radioactive sample to decay. Henri Becquerel discovered radioactivity in 1896. Marie and Pierre Curie isolated radium and polonium. Radioactive isotopes are used in carbon dating, medical imaging, and cancer radiotherapy. Nuclear power generates electricity through controlled fission. Radioactive waste requires long-term storage. Applications include nuclear medicine, archaeological dating, and energy production.

TECTONIC PLATES
Plate tectonics explains the movement of Earth's lithospheric plates and accounts for the distribution of continents, oceans, mountains, earthquakes, and volcanoes. Alfred Wegener proposed continental drift in 1912 but could not explain the mechanism. Seafloor spreading discovered in the 1950s provided the answer. Harry Hess proposed that convection currents in the mantle drive plate movement. Divergent boundaries create new crust at mid-ocean ridges. Convergent boundaries form mountains and subduction zones. Transform boundaries cause earthquakes as plates slide past each other. The theory is as fundamental to geology as evolution is to biology. Applications include earthquake prediction, resource exploration, and understanding Earth's history.

SLEEP
Sleep is a naturally recurring state of reduced consciousness and sensory activity essential for health. Sleep occurs in cycles of non-REM and REM sleep. REM sleep features rapid eye movements, vivid dreaming, and muscle paralysis. Deep non-REM sleep is critical for physical restoration and memory consolidation. The circadian rhythm regulates sleep-wake cycles responding to light through the suprachiasmatic nucleus. Sleep deprivation impairs cognition, immune function, and emotional regulation. Sleep disorders include insomnia, sleep apnea, and narcolepsy. The functions of sleep remain incompletely understood but include waste clearance from the brain via the glymphatic system. Applications include sleep medicine, shift work management, and mental health treatment.

NATURAL SELECTION
Natural selection is the differential survival and reproduction of individuals due to differences in traits. Charles Darwin and Alfred Russel Wallace independently proposed the theory in 1858. Variation exists among individuals. Variation is heritable. Certain variants produce more offspring. Over generations, favorable traits become more common. Natural selection acts on individuals but populations evolve. Adaptations are traits shaped by selection for specific functions. Sexual selection drives the evolution of traits that improve mating success. Artificial selection by humans demonstrates the power of selective breeding. Natural selection is not random and does not produce perfection. Applications include understanding antibiotic resistance, crop breeding, and conservation biology.

ENZYMES
Enzymes are protein catalysts that accelerate biochemical reactions without being consumed. Each enzyme has an active site specific to its substrate. The lock and key model describes this specificity. The induced fit model accounts for conformational changes upon binding. Enzymes lower activation energy, allowing reactions to proceed at physiological temperatures. Enzyme activity depends on temperature, pH, and substrate concentration. Cofactors like vitamins and metal ions assist enzyme function. Enzyme inhibition can be competitive, noncompetitive, or uncompetitive. Many drugs work by inhibiting specific enzymes. Applications include pharmaceutical development, industrial biotechnology, and diagnostic testing.

PLANETARY MOTION
Johannes Kepler described three laws of planetary motion in the early 1600s using Tycho Brahe's observations. Planets orbit the Sun in ellipses with the Sun at one focus. A line from planet to Sun sweeps out equal areas in equal time. The square of the orbital period is proportional to the cube of the semi-major axis. Newton later derived Kepler's laws from his law of universal gravitation, showing they follow from an inverse-square force. These laws apply to moons, comets, and artificial satellites. They enable calculation of orbital trajectories and masses of celestial bodies. Applications include space mission planning, satellite deployment, and exoplanet detection.

RESPIRATION
Cellular respiration is the process by which cells convert glucose and oxygen into ATP, the energy currency of life. Glycolysis in the cytoplasm splits glucose into pyruvate. The citric acid cycle in mitochondria completes the oxidation, releasing carbon dioxide. The electron transport chain produces most ATP through oxidative phosphorylation. Hans Krebs discovered the citric acid cycle in 1937. Aerobic respiration yields about thirty-six ATP per glucose molecule. Anaerobic respiration, like in sprinting muscles, yields only two ATP and produces lactic acid. Respiration connects biochemistry to physiology and sports science. Applications include understanding metabolism, mitochondrial diseases, and athletic performance.

BACTERIA
Bacteria are single-celled prokaryotic microorganisms found in virtually every environment on Earth. They lack a nucleus and membrane-bound organelles. Cell walls contain peptidoglycan in most species, targeted by antibiotics. Bacteria reproduce asexually through binary fission. They exchange genetic material through conjugation, transformation, and transduction, contributing to antibiotic resistance. Beneficial bacteria in the human gut aid digestion and immune function. Pathogenic bacteria cause diseases including tuberculosis, cholera, and strep throat. Antonie van Leeuwenhoek first observed bacteria in the 1670s. Louis Pasteur and Robert Koch established the germ theory of disease. Applications include probiotics, bioremediation, industrial fermentation, and genetic engineering.

FUNGI
Fungi are a kingdom of eukaryotic organisms including yeasts, molds, and mushrooms. They are heterotrophs that absorb nutrients by secreting digestive enzymes onto their food sources externally. Cell walls contain chitin, not cellulose as in plants. Fungi reproduce through spores sexually and asexually. Mycorrhizal fungi form symbiotic relationships with plant roots, enhancing nutrient uptake. Lichens are symbiotic partnerships between fungi and algae or cyanobacteria. Penicillium fungi gave us the first antibiotic. Saccharomyces cerevisiae, brewer's yeast, enables bread and alcohol production. Fungi are the primary decomposers in many ecosystems. Applications include medicine, food production, bioremediation, and materials science.

OXYGEN
The discovery of oxygen in the 1770s by Joseph Priestley and Carl Wilhelm Scheele transformed chemistry by overturning the phlogiston theory. Antoine Lavoisier named the element and demonstrated its role in combustion and respiration. Oxygen is the third most abundant element in the universe and makes up twenty-one percent of Earth's atmosphere. It is highly reactive, forming compounds with most elements. Aerobic organisms depend on oxygen for efficient energy production. The Great Oxidation Event about 2.4 billion years ago transformed Earth's atmosphere as photosynthetic cyanobacteria produced oxygen. Reactive oxygen species can damage cells, countered by antioxidants. Applications include medical oxygen therapy, steel manufacturing, and rocket propulsion.

NUCLEAR FISSION
Nuclear fission splits heavy atomic nuclei like uranium into lighter elements, releasing enormous energy. Lise Meitner and Otto Hahn discovered fission in 1938. When a neutron strikes a fissile nucleus, the nucleus becomes unstable and splits. Additional neutrons are released, enabling a chain reaction. Controlled chain reactions sustain nuclear reactors for electricity generation. Uncontrolled chain reactions produce nuclear explosions. The mass lost during fission is converted to energy as described by E equals mc squared. Nuclear power provides carbon-free baseload electricity but produces long-lived radioactive waste. Applications include nuclear power plants, naval propulsion, medical isotope production, and nuclear weapons.

OCEAN CURRENTS
Ocean currents are continuous directed movements of seawater driven by wind, temperature, salinity, and Earth's rotation. Surface currents like the Gulf Stream are primarily wind-driven. Deep ocean currents are driven by density differences, forming the global thermohaline circulation or great ocean conveyor belt. The Coriolis effect deflects currents to the right in the Northern Hemisphere and left in the Southern Hemisphere. Upwelling brings nutrient-rich deep water to the surface, supporting productive fisheries. Ocean currents transport heat from the equator toward the poles, regulating global climate. El Nino and La Nina are periodic changes in Pacific Ocean currents with worldwide weather impacts. Applications include climate prediction, shipping route optimization, and marine conservation.

VACCINES
Vaccines stimulate the immune system to develop protection against specific pathogens without causing disease. Edward Jenner developed the first vaccine against smallpox in 1796 using cowpox virus. Vaccines present antigens from pathogens to train adaptive immunity and create memory cells. Types include live attenuated, inactivated, subunit, toxoid, and mRNA vaccines. Herd immunity protects vulnerable individuals when enough of the population is immunized. Vaccination eradicated smallpox and has nearly eliminated polio. mRNA vaccine technology proved crucial during the COVID-19 pandemic. Vaccine hesitancy fueled by misinformation threatens public health gains. Applications include pandemic preparedness, cancer immunotherapy, and veterinary medicine.

MAGNETISM
Magnetism is a force arising from the motion of electric charges. Every magnet has a north and south pole. Like poles repel, opposite poles attract. Iron, nickel, and cobalt are ferromagnetic materials. Earth's magnetic field is generated by the motion of molten iron in the outer core, the geodynamo. The magnetic field protects Earth from solar wind and cosmic radiation. The aurora borealis and australis occur when charged particles interact with the magnetic field near the poles. Magnetic field reversals have occurred many times in Earth's history. William Gilbert established the scientific study of magnetism in 1600. Applications include electric motors, data storage, medical MRI, and navigation.

BIODIVERSITY
Biodiversity describes the variety of life at genetic, species, and ecosystem levels. High biodiversity increases ecosystem resilience and productivity. Tropical rainforests and coral reefs are biodiversity hotspots. Habitat loss, climate change, pollution, overexploitation, and invasive species drive biodiversity loss. The current extinction rate is estimated to be hundreds of times higher than the natural background rate, prompting designation of the Anthropocene as a sixth mass extinction. Conservation strategies include protected areas, habitat restoration, and captive breeding programs. E.O. Wilson popularized the term biodiversity in the 1980s. Applications include conservation biology, ecosystem services valuation, and sustainable development policy.

HORMONES
Hormones are chemical messengers secreted by endocrine glands that travel through the bloodstream to target organs. Insulin from the pancreas lowers blood glucose. Thyroid hormones regulate metabolism. Cortisol mediates stress response. Estrogen and testosterone control reproduction and secondary sex characteristics. Growth hormone stimulates growth and cell reproduction. Melatonin from the pineal gland regulates sleep cycles. Hormone levels are controlled by negative feedback loops. Endocrine disruptors are environmental chemicals that interfere with hormone function. Ernest Starling coined the term hormone in 1905. Applications include endocrinology, reproductive medicine, and treatment of metabolic disorders.

BLACK HOLES
Black holes are regions of spacetime where gravity is so strong that nothing, not even light, can escape. They form when massive stars collapse at the end of their lives. The event horizon is the boundary beyond which escape is impossible. The singularity at the center is a point of infinite density where known physics breaks down. Karl Schwarzschild found the first solution to Einstein's equations describing a black hole in 1916. The Event Horizon Telescope captured the first image of a black hole in 2019, showing the shadow of M87's supermassive black hole. Hawking radiation predicts black holes slowly evaporate. Applications include testing general relativity, understanding galaxy formation, and exploring quantum gravity.

CLIMATE CHANGE
Climate change refers to long-term shifts in global temperature and weather patterns. The current warming is primarily caused by human emissions of greenhouse gases from fossil fuel combustion, deforestation, and agriculture. Global average temperature has risen about 1.2 degrees Celsius since pre-industrial times. Effects include rising sea levels, more intense storms, heat waves, droughts, and ecosystem disruption. Climate models project further warming depending on emission scenarios. The Intergovernmental Panel on Climate Change assesses scientific consensus. Mitigation strategies include renewable energy, carbon pricing, and reforestation. Adaptation includes sea walls, drought-resistant crops, and disaster preparedness. Applications include energy policy, urban planning, and international climate agreements.

MENDELIAN GENETICS
Gregor Mendel discovered the basic principles of inheritance in 1865 through experiments with pea plants. Traits are determined by discrete units now called genes. Organisms inherit two copies of each gene, one from each parent. Dominant alleles mask recessive alleles in heterozygotes. The law of segregation states that allele pairs separate during gamete formation. The law of independent assortment states that genes for different traits assort independently during gamete formation. Mendel's work was rediscovered in 1900 by Hugo de Vries, Carl Correns, and Erich von Tschermak. Mendelian genetics underlies our understanding of inherited diseases, genetic counseling, and breeding programs. Applications include predicting inheritance patterns, understanding genetic disorders, and agricultural genetics.

CARDIOVASCULAR SYSTEM
The cardiovascular system delivers oxygenated blood from the heart through arteries to tissues and returns deoxygenated blood through veins to the heart and lungs. The heart has four chambers: right atrium, right ventricle, left atrium, left ventricle. The sinoatrial node acts as the natural pacemaker generating electrical impulses. Blood pressure is maintained through vessel constriction and dilation. Atherosclerosis narrows arteries through plaque buildup, leading to heart attacks and strokes. Exercise strengthens cardiovascular function. The system connects to respiratory, endocrine, and nervous systems. Applications include cardiology, cardiovascular surgery, and preventive medicine.

ROCK CYCLE
The rock cycle describes how rocks form, break down, and transform between igneous, sedimentary, and metamorphic types. Igneous rocks crystallize from cooling magma or lava. Sedimentary rocks form from compressed sediments or precipitated minerals. Metamorphic rocks result from heat and pressure transforming existing rock. Weathering and erosion break rocks down into sediments. Plate tectonics drives the rock cycle by subducting, uplifting, and melting crust. James Hutton established the principle of uniformitarianism in the late 1700s, recognizing the rock cycle operates over geological time. Applications include mining geology, fossil fuel exploration, and understanding Earth's history.

CHROMOSOMES
Chromosomes are threadlike structures of DNA and protein found in the nucleus of eukaryotic cells. Humans have 23 pairs, 46 total chromosomes. Each chromosome contains hundreds to thousands of genes. The centromere divides chromosomes into short and long arms. Telomeres cap chromosome ends and shorten with each cell division, contributing to aging. Chromosomal abnormalities cause genetic disorders. Down syndrome results from three copies of chromosome 21. Karyotyping visualizes chromosome number and structure. Applications include prenatal testing, cancer cytogenetics, and evolutionary biology.
